section.title: The Vision of the Four Beasts
section.sectionRationale: The book turns from court narratives to Daniel's own visions, and this first vision is the hinge of the whole book. Four monstrous beasts rise from the sea, a blasphemous little horn wages war on the saints, and then the Ancient of Days convenes His court and gives an everlasting kingdom to one like a son of man and to the saints of the Most High. It re-tells the four-kingdom scheme of chapter 2 from heaven's side, with judgment and the triumph of God's people at its center.
section.thesis: History's brutal empires rise like beasts from the churning sea and a defiant horn oppresses the saints for an appointed season, but the Ancient of Days holds court, strips the beasts of power, and gives an everlasting, indestructible kingdom to one like a son of man and to the saints of the Most High.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the four beasts from the sea; the Ancient of Days seated for judgment; one like the Son of Man given everlasting dominion; the interpretation, four kings but the saints receive the kingdom; and the fourth beast, the little horn, and the everlasting kingdom of the saints.

units.label: The four beasts from the sea

verses.text: In the first year of the reign of Belshazzar over Babylon, Daniel had a dream, and visions passed through his mind as he lay on his bed. He wrote down the dream, and this is the summary of his account.
Daniel declared: “In my vision in the night I looked, and suddenly the four winds of heaven were churning up the great sea.
Then four great beasts came up out of the sea, each one different from the others:

The first beast was like a lion, and it had the wings of an eagle. I watched until its wings were torn off and it was lifted up from the ground and made to stand on two feet like a man and given the mind of a man.

Suddenly another beast appeared, which looked like a bear. It was raised up on one of its sides, and it had three ribs in its mouth between its teeth. So it was told, ‘Get up and gorge yourself on flesh!’
Next, as I watched, suddenly another beast appeared. It was like a leopard, and on its back it had four wings like those of a bird. The beast also had four heads, and it was given authority to rule.

After this, as I watched in my vision in the night, suddenly a fourth beast appeared, and it was terrifying, dreadful and extremely strong, with large iron teeth. It devoured and crushed; then it trampled underfoot whatever was left. It was different from all the beasts before it, and it had ten horns.

While I was contemplating the horns, suddenly another horn, a little one, came up among them, and three of the first horns were uprooted before it. This horn had eyes like those of a man and a mouth that spoke words of arrogance.

units.label: The Ancient of Days seated for judgment

verses.text: As I continued to watch, thrones were set in place, and the {{attiq_yomin|Ancient of Days}} took His seat. His clothing was white as snow, and the hair of His head was like pure wool. His throne was flaming with fire, and its wheels were all ablaze.
A river of fire was flowing, coming out from His presence. Thousands upon thousands attended Him, and myriads upon myriads stood before Him. The court was convened, and the books were opened.
Then I kept watching because of the arrogant words the horn was speaking. As I continued to watch, the beast was slain, and its body was destroyed and thrown into the blazing fire.
As for the rest of the beasts, their dominion was removed, but they were granted an extension of life for a season and a time.

units.label: One like the Son of Man

verses.text: In my vision in the night I continued to watch, and I saw One like the {{bar_enash|Son of Man}} coming with the clouds of heaven. He approached the Ancient of Days and was led into His presence.

And He was given {{shaltan|dominion}}, glory, and kingship, that the people of every nation and language should serve Him. His dominion is an everlasting dominion that will not pass away, and His kingdom is one that will never be destroyed.

units.label: The interpretation: the saints receive the kingdom
units.note: four kings, but the saints inherit

verses.text: I, Daniel, was grieved in my spirit, and the visions in my mind alarmed me.
I approached one of those who were standing there, and I asked him the true meaning of all this. So he told me the interpretation of these things:
‘These four great beasts are four kings who will arise from the earth.
But the {{qaddishin|saints}} of the Most High will receive the kingdom and possess it forever, yes, forever and ever.’

Then I wanted to know the true meaning of the fourth beast, which was different from all the others, extremely terrifying, devouring and crushing with iron teeth and bronze claws, then trampling underfoot whatever was left.

I also wanted to know about the ten horns on its head and the other horn that came up, before which three of them fell, the horn whose appearance was more imposing than the others, with eyes and with a mouth that spoke words of arrogance.
As I watched, this horn was waging war against the saints and prevailing against them,
until the Ancient of Days arrived and pronounced judgment in favor of the saints of the Most High, and the time came for them to possess the kingdom.

units.label: The fourth beast and the everlasting kingdom

verses.text: This is what he said: ‘The fourth beast is a fourth kingdom that will appear on the earth, different from all the other kingdoms, and it will devour the whole earth, trample it down, and crush it.
And the ten horns are ten kings who will rise from this kingdom. After them another king, different from the earlier ones, will rise and subdue three kings.

He will speak out against the Most High and oppress the saints of the Most High, intending to change the appointed times and laws; and the saints will be given into his hand for a time, and times, and half a time.
But the court will convene, and his dominion will be taken away and completely destroyed forever.

Then the sovereignty, dominion, and greatness of the kingdoms under all of heaven will be given to the people, the saints of the Most High. His kingdom will be an everlasting kingdom, and all rulers will serve and obey Him.’
Thus ends the matter. As for me, Daniel, my thoughts troubled me greatly, and my face turned pale. But I kept the matter to myself.”

attiq_yomin.gloss: The Ancient of Days, literally the one advanced in days. It is a title for God as the eternal Judge, seated on a fiery throne with countless attendants and the books of judgment open. Against the raging beasts of history, He is the calm, everlasting King who convenes the court and settles every account.

bar_enash.gloss: One like a son of man, a human figure in contrast to the beasts. He comes with the clouds of heaven, a mark of deity, and receives everlasting dominion from the Ancient of Days. Jesus made this His favorite self-title, and at His trial He applied this very verse to Himself; the phrase names both His true humanity and His divine, universal reign.

shaltan.gloss: Dominion, authority, rule. The beasts are given a limited shaltan that is stripped away, but the son of man is given an everlasting shaltan that will never pass away or be destroyed. The word contrasts the passing power of empires with the unshakable reign handed to Christ and shared with His people.

qaddishin.gloss: The holy ones, the saints. The saints of the Most High are oppressed by the arrogant horn for an appointed season, but the court finds in their favor and they receive and possess the everlasting kingdom. The word gathers God's suffering people into the triumph of the son of man, who reigns and shares His kingdom with them.

basic.context: Daniel now becomes the one who dreams. He sees four terrifying beasts rise from a stormy sea, the last with ten horns and a small, boastful horn that attacks God's people. Then the scene shifts to heaven: the Ancient of Days takes His throne, a court is seated, the books are opened, and the beast is judged. One like a son of man comes on the clouds and is given an everlasting kingdom, and the saints of the Most High are told they will receive and possess that kingdom forever.
basic.hermeneutics: This vision covers the same ground as chapter 2's statue, four kingdoms replaced by God's everlasting reign, but seen from heaven, as a courtroom drama of judgment and vindication. The details of the beasts and horns are debated, and wise readers hold them humbly. The unmistakable center is clear: God judges the proud powers, the son of man reigns forever, and the persecuted saints inherit the kingdom.
basic.originalLanguages: The eternal Judge is the attiq yomin, the Ancient of Days. The human figure who receives everlasting rule is the bar enash, one like a son of man. His shaltan, dominion, never passes away, unlike the beasts. And the qaddishin, the saints of the Most High, share in that everlasting kingdom.
basic.typology: The one like a son of man, coming on the clouds to receive an everlasting kingdom, is fulfilled in Jesus, who took this title as His own. The heavenly court where the books are opened foreshadows the last judgment, and the vindication of the persecuted saints anticipates the final triumph of God's people in Christ.
basic.christ: Jesus called Himself the Son of Man more than any other title, and at His trial He quoted this very scene, claiming to be the one who comes on the clouds to reign. The everlasting dominion given here is His. He is both the human representative of God's people and the divine King, and He shares His kingdom with the saints who suffered with Him.
basic.god: God is the Ancient of Days, the eternal Judge enthroned above the chaos of history. However fierce the beasts, however loud the boasting horn, God sits in unshaken majesty, convenes His court, and gives the verdict. He limits the time of evil, vindicates His people, and hands the everlasting kingdom to His Son.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel 2 tells the same four kingdoms as a statue. Matthew 26 records Jesus applying the son of man coming on the clouds to Himself. And Revelation 1 and 5 show the risen Christ with hair white as wool, receiving the kingdom and reigning forever.
basic.oneStory: History looks like a sea of monsters, one empire devouring the next, and God's people crushed under a blasphemous power. But heaven's court has the last word: the beasts are judged, the Son of Man is crowned, and the saints inherit an everlasting kingdom. This is the hope of the whole Bible, fulfilled in Jesus, the Son of Man who reigns and will share His reign with all who are His.

context.literary: The pivot of the book, joining the Aramaic court narratives, which it ends, to the visions that follow. It mirrors the four-kingdom dream of chapter 2 but reframes it as an apocalyptic courtroom scene, rich in symbol: beasts, horns, thrones, and clouds. The vision's center of gravity is the twin scenes of the Ancient of Days and the son of man.
context.historical: Written for exiles under Gentile empires, the vision assures them that the brutal kingdoms of the world, and any future tyrant who defies God and persecutes His people, are under God's judgment. The imagery of beasts from the sea draws on ancient pictures of chaos, and the little horn who changes times and laws has often been connected with a specific persecuting ruler.
context.canonical: Daniel 7 is one of the most quoted chapters in the New Testament. The title Son of Man, coming with the clouds, and the everlasting kingdom given to the saints shape the teaching of Jesus and the visions of Revelation. The Ancient of Days imagery reappears describing the glorified Christ, and the everlasting kingdom is the goal of the whole biblical story.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal that above the violent succession of world empires God reigns as the Ancient of Days, that He will judge every proud and persecuting power, and that He gives an everlasting kingdom to one like a son of man and to the suffering saints, so that God's people endure in hope.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision describes what God will do in history and at its end; it is not a coded calendar for date-setting. Its abiding truths are God's sovereign judgment over evil empires, the everlasting reign of the son of man, and the certain vindication of persecuted saints, calling believers to faithful endurance.

debates.question: Who is the one like a son of man in verse 13?

views.label: The Messiah, a divine and human King
views.case: He comes with the clouds of heaven, a divine prerogative, receives worship and an everlasting kingdom, and is presented as an individual; Jesus applied this very verse to Himself at His trial, so Christians read Him as the Messiah, both truly human and truly divine.

views.label: A representative of the saints, corporately
views.case: The interpretation says the saints of the Most High receive the kingdom, so some read the son of man as a symbol for God's people, the human figure standing over against the beastly empires; on this view the individual and corporate readings converge in the King who embodies His people.

debates.bearing: The New Testament resolves the two: the Son of Man is Jesus, the divine-human King who reigns forever and includes His people in His reign, so that His victory is theirs.

debates.question: What is the fourth beast and its little horn?

views.label: Rome and a later blasphemous power
views.case: Read alongside chapter 2's iron kingdom, the fourth beast is often identified with Rome, and the little horn with a future antichrist figure who wars on the saints for a time, times, and half a time before God's court destroys him, a pattern Revelation develops.

views.label: Greece and Antiochus IV
views.case: Reading Media and Persia as separate beasts makes the fourth beast Greece, and the little horn Antiochus IV Epiphanes, who desecrated the temple and persecuted the Jews, changing the sacred times and laws, before his power was broken.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree on the point that matters: a defiant power will oppress God's people for a limited, appointed time, and then the heavenly court will strip away its dominion and give the everlasting kingdom to the saints.

study.originalLanguages: The vision sets two figures at its heart. The attiq yomin, the Ancient of Days, is the eternal Judge on a throne of fire, before whom the books are opened and the boastful beast is condemned. Then the bar enash, one like a son of man, comes with the clouds of heaven, a movement reserved for God, and receives shaltan, dominion, that is everlasting and can never be destroyed, unlike the fleeting rule of the beasts. And the qaddishin, the saints of the Most High, though oppressed by the arrogant horn for a time, times, and half a time, are vindicated by the court and given the kingdom to possess forever. Human wickedness rages like a beast; the true and lasting reign is human in the best sense, the son of man and his holy people.

typology.type: One like a son of man coming on the clouds
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the Son of Man, who applied this scene to Himself and will come on the clouds in glory, Matthew 26:64 and Mark 13:26.

typology.type: The Ancient of Days and the opened books
typology.fulfillment: The final judgment before God's throne, where the books are opened, Revelation 20:11-12.

typology.type: The persecuted saints who receive the kingdom
typology.fulfillment: The people of God who suffer with Christ and reign with Him forever, Romans 8:17 and Revelation 22:5.

study.christ: Of all His titles, Jesus most often called Himself the Son of Man, drawing on this very vision; and standing on trial before the high priest, He declared that they would see the Son of Man coming with the clouds of heaven, applying Daniel 7 directly to Himself. The everlasting dominion, glory, and kingship given here are His by right, received from the Ancient of Days and exercised over every nation and language. He is at once the true human, standing over against the beastly powers, and the divine King worthy of the worship of all peoples. And He does not reign alone: the saints who suffered under the horn are given the kingdom with Him, sharing the reign of the Son of Man forever.
study.god: God is the Ancient of Days, enthroned in fire above the churning sea of history, unmoved by the roaring of empires and the boasting of tyrants. He convenes the court, opens the books, and pronounces judgment, setting a strict limit on the time evil is allowed and then stripping the beast of its dominion forever. He vindicates His oppressed people and hands the everlasting kingdom to His Son and, through Him, to the saints. History is not chaos; it is under the gavel of a sovereign and righteous Judge.

crossReferences.note: The same four kingdoms seen as a statue, ending in God's everlasting kingdom, here retold from heaven's side.
Jesus applies the Son of Man coming with the clouds to Himself before the high priest, claiming this vision.
The glorified Christ described with hair white as wool, blending the Son of Man and the Ancient of Days.
The throne of judgment and the opened books, the final court foreseen in Daniel 7.

study.oneStory: The nations of history rise like monsters from a stormy sea, and a blasphemous power tramples the saints, but the vision lifts our eyes to heaven's courtroom, where the Ancient of Days sits enthroned and the books are opened. The beasts are judged, and one like a son of man is crowned with an everlasting kingdom that He shares with His suffering people. This is the hope that carries the whole Bible: Jesus, the Son of Man, reigns forever, and all who are His will possess the kingdom with Him, world without end.

quiz.q: What do the four beasts rising from the sea represent?

options.text: Four kings or kingdoms that arise from the earth, a succession of worldly empires.
options.note: Daniel 7:17 interprets the beasts as four kings who arise.

options.text: Four literal animals sent to punish Babylon.
options.note: They are symbols of kingdoms, not literal animals.

options.text: Four false gods of the nations.
options.note: They picture political kingdoms, not gods.

options.text: The four seasons of the year.
options.note: The beasts stand for successive empires, not seasons.

quiz.q: Who is the Ancient of Days in the vision?

options.text: God, the eternal Judge, enthroned in fire, who convenes the court and opens the books.
options.note: Daniel 7:9-10 pictures God as the everlasting Judge over history.

options.text: The oldest of the four kings.
options.note: He is not a king of the beasts but God Himself, seated in judgment over them.

options.text: An angel who guards the throne.
options.note: He is God the Judge, not merely an attending angel.

options.text: The little horn grown old.
options.note: The horn is judged by the Ancient of Days, who is God.

quiz.q: What is given to the one like a son of man?

options.text: Everlasting dominion, glory, and a kingdom that will never be destroyed, served by all peoples.
options.note: Daniel 7:14 describes his eternal, universal reign.

options.text: A temporary rule that will later pass to another.
options.note: The opposite; his dominion will not pass away and his kingdom cannot be destroyed.

options.text: Command over the four beasts as their leader.
options.note: He receives an everlasting kingdom from God, not leadership of the beasts.

options.text: Only the land of Judah.
options.note: His dominion is over every nation and language, not one land.

quiz.q: What happens to the saints of the Most High in the end?

options.text: After being oppressed for an appointed time, they are vindicated and receive the everlasting kingdom.
options.note: Daniel 7:18, 22, 27 promise the saints will possess the kingdom forever.

options.text: They are destroyed along with the beasts.
options.note: The opposite; they inherit the kingdom after their suffering.

options.text: They are left out of the kingdom entirely.
options.note: They receive and possess the kingdom forever.

options.text: They become the fifth beast.
options.note: They are God's holy people, given the kingdom, not another beast.

quiz.q: How does the Son of Man vision point to Jesus?

options.text: Jesus took the title Son of Man and applied this coming with the clouds to Himself, claiming the everlasting kingdom.
options.note: Jesus quotes Daniel 7:13 at His trial, Matthew 26:64.

options.text: It describes a purely symbolic figure with no link to any person.
options.note: The New Testament identifies the Son of Man as the real, personal Christ.

options.text: It predicts a Roman emperor who would help the Jews.
options.note: The everlasting, worshiped King is Christ, not a Roman emperor.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: Jesus explicitly claimed this vision as speaking of Himself.

questions: Daniel saw history as a sea of beasts, yet God enthroned above it all. When the news feels like one monster after another, how does the throne of the Ancient of Days steady you?
The boastful horn was given only a set time, times, and half a time. How does knowing that evil has an appointed limit shape the way you endure hard seasons?
The Son of Man reigns forever and shares His kingdom with the saints. What difference does it make to know that suffering with Christ leads to reigning with Him?
Heaven's court opened the books and gave the verdict. How does the certainty of God's final judgment comfort you, and how does it sober you?
Daniel was deeply troubled by what he saw, yet he trusted God. How do you hold on to faith when God's ways in history are frightening or hard to understand?

translationNotes.note: The great sea may refer to the Mediterranean, but as apocalyptic imagery the churning sea pictures the restless chaos of the nations from which empires arise.
One like the Son of Man may be rendered one like a son of man; the New Testament repeatedly applies this coming with the clouds to Jesus, as the cross references show.
A time, and times, and half a time is a symbolic span, often taken as three and a half units, denoting a limited period of persecution set by God, echoed in Revelation.
The verse marks the close of the Aramaic section of Daniel, which began at 2:4b; from chapter 8 the book returns to Hebrew.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Daniel 7:1-28. Em dashes in verses 7, 18, 19, and 20 converted to commas. Poetic lines rendered as flowing prose. Chapter 7 completes the Aramaic section, so load-bearing lexicon entries use Aramaic. No em or en dashes used.
